coredata.dc:title: The remarkable enhancement of photo-stability and antioxidant protection of lutein coupled with carbon-dot
coredata.dc:creator: Jv, Da-jian
Ji, Tian-hao
Xu, Zhemi
Li, Ao
Chen, Zhong-yi
coredata.dc:description: Lutein is a carotenoid that is beneficial to human health. However, its low stability and bioavailability have limited its application in the pharmaceutical and food industries. Herein, lutein has been successfully modified with carbon-dots (CDs) via a simple and mild solvent-ultrasonic method at room temperature. The synthesized lutein/CDs composites (LCs) showed 3.4 times higher photostability than the pristine lutein under UV irradiation, and 3.5 times higher than that under visible light, and the retention rate of lutein in the air rose from 3.6 % to 68 % over 25 days. Meanwhile, the antioxidant capacity of lutein has been improved by 6.4 times. Based on the photoluminescence (PL) measurements, a possible mechanism for the enhanced photostability and antioxidant ability of LCs has been proposed. The work provides a simple but effective approach to enhancing the stability and antioxidant capacity of lutein, and is expected to extend its application in food and biomedical fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lutein
Carbon dots
Stability
Antioxidant ability

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Agricultural Environmental Monitoring Station of Beijing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the funding of the Agricultural Environmental Monitoring Station of Beijing (No. 19000531813, 19000551279, and 19000551347) and Chemistry and Material Engineering College in BTBU.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FTIR spectra; (B) XRD patterns; (C) contact angles of lutein, CDs, LC0.5 (33.3% (w/w) CDs), LC1(50% (w/w) CDs), LC2 (66.7% (w/w) CDs) and LC3 (75% (w/w) CDs).

(A) The color fading pictures of lutein, LC0.5, LC1, LC2, and LC3 solutions under UV irradiation with different exposed durations. (B) Photostability of lutein, CDs, and LCs with an original concentration of 0.04 mg/mL under visible light, (C) UV irradiation. (D) and (E) Photostability presented in ln(C0/Ct) under visible light and UV irradiation. (F) and (G) Kinetic constants indicating the degradation rate under visible light and UV irradiation.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of lutein, LC0.5, LC1, LC2, LC3. Inset in (A) is UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs. (B) PL spectra of lutein, CDs, LC3 under excitation at 447 nm. (C) PL spectra of LC0.5, LC1, LC2, LC3 under excitation at 447 nm. All the samples were measured under the same conditions.
The retention rate of lutein, CDs, LC0.5, LC1, LC2, LC3 exposed in the air in the dark.
DPPH radical scavenging rate of lutein, CDs, LCs, and L-Ch.
Proposed mechanisms of DPPH free radical scavenged by LCs.

head.ce:title: The remarkable enhancement of photo-stability and antioxidant protection of lutein coupled with carbon-dot

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Material Engineering College, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Material Engineering College, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lutein is a carotenoid that is beneficial to human health. However, its low stability and bioavailability have limited its application in the pharmaceutical and food industries. Herein, lutein has been successfully modified with carbon-dots (CDs) via a simple and mild solvent-ultrasonic method at room temperature. The synthesized lutein/CDs composites (LCs) showed 3.4 times higher photostability than the pristine lutein under UV irradiation, and 3.5 times higher than that under visible light, and the retention rate of lutein in the air rose from 3.6 % to 68 % over 25 days. Meanwhile, the antioxidant capacity of lutein has been improved by 6.4 times. Based on the photoluminescence (PL) measurements, a possible mechanism for the enhanced photostability and antioxidant ability of LCs has been proposed. The work provides a simple but effective approach to enhancing the stability and antioxidant capacity of lutein, and is expected to extend its application in food and biomedical fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most common carotenoids, lutein is vital for the human organs as it can prevent various diseases, such as age-related macular degeneration, the disorder of eyes, cardiovascular disease, cancer prevention, etc., owing to its antioxidant effects (Ahn & Kim, 2021; Gong et al., 2018; Johnson, 2014; Mares, 2016; Obana et al., 2021). Lutein can be only acquired via diet since it cannot be synthesized in vivo by humans (Granado et al., 2003). However, the lipophilic lutein with low water solubility leads to low absorption in the gastrointestinal tract, compromising its bioavailability (Gumus et al., 2016; Murillo et al., 2016; Xia et al, 2012). In addition, lutein is also sensitive to light, oxygen, and other environments such as heat, pH, etc., because of its conjugated alkene structure, and thus limited its retention (Kuang et al., 2015; Qv et al., 2011). Meanwhile, its antioxidant ability needs to be further improved as lutein may cause skin-yellowing (carotenoderma) when taken in excess (Buscemi et al., 2018). Hence, improving the bioavailability, stability, and antioxidant capacity of lutein is critical for its applications in the pharmaceutical and food industries.

Encapsulation, microemulsion, esterification, and heterostructure are four main techniques to improve the bioavailability, stability, and antioxidant ability of lutein. Encapsulation can protect the nutrient of food from the environment, and also realized controllable release or prolong the residence of core compounds in the human body. For example, the fabrication of nanoencapsulation with chitosan/γ-PGA effectively improved the water solubility of lutein (Hong et al., 2016). Huang et al. (2019) developed lutein-alginate microspheres, which showed better stability and antioxidant capacity due to the interaction between alginate and lutein. Microemulsions consist of both aqueous and oil phases counterbalancing through the surfactant, which has both polar and non-polar phases and thus shows potential as a nanocarrier with good water solubility. For instance, lutein microemulsion has been reported to have high water dispersibility and better stability under stress conditions such as UV irradiation and heat, which is owing to the efficient encapsulation of lutein by the wall material (Jalali-Jivan et al., 2019; Jalali-Jivan et al., 2021). Similarly, microemulsions can be used as edible nutrient carriers to enhance the storage stability and light stability of lycopene (Amiri-Rigi & Abbasi, 2020; Amiri-Rigi & Abbasi, 2017). Esterification is the chemical modification of lutein at the molecular level to enhance the bioavailability of lutein by changing the functional groups. Lujan-Montelongo et al. (2018) proposed an enzymatic approach for the regioselective synthesis of 3-O-acyl monoester lutein derivatives, greatly improving the bioavailability of lutein. Fabrication of heterostructures can also enhance the stability and antioxidant ability of lutein by the interaction between different substances. Li et al. (2020) incorporated lutein into layered double hydroxide (LDH), showing remarkable enhancement of the thermal and light stability of lutein owing to the strong chemical interaction between lutein and LDH (Li et al., 2020). It has been demonstrated that loading lutein on graphene oxide can significantly enhance the free radical scavenging capacity of lutein through the interaction between lutein and graphene oxide (Chae et al., 2021).

However, it should not be ignored that many modification strategies of lutein still need to be further optimized due to their complicated preparation processes, high-toxic (such as surfactant), by-products, and pollution, which may significantly limit the applications of lutein. Therefore, it is necessary to develop greener, safer, and more efficient strategies to improve the bioavailability, stability, and antioxidant ability of lutein. Carbon-dots (CDs) have recently emerged as a promising class of drug carriers for various biomedical applications (Ashrafizadeh et al., 2020; Hettiarachchi et al., 2019; Samantara et al., 2016). The drug can be loaded on the hydrophilic CDs through π-π staking to improve stability and solubility (Yuan et al., 2016). Generally, CDs are discrete poly-molecules with a diameter of less than 10 nm, which exhibit unique physical and chemical properties, such as high-water solubility, good photostability, low toxicity, easy modification, and low manufacturing cost, etc. (Baker & Baker, 2010; Das et al., 2018; Javed & O'Carroll, 2021; Lim et al., 2018). Notably, CDs have also been reported as good antioxidant agents in free radical scavenging (Zhang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2015). Therefore, CDs is expected to be employed to improve the solubility, stability, and antioxidant capacity of lutein, which has not been reported so far.

Motivated by the application limitations of the lutein in the pharmaceutical and food industries, herein, we report a modification route of lutein with the highly stable and soluble CDs via a simple solvent-ultrasonic method at room temperature. The photostability and antioxidant capacity of lutein have been significantly enhanced by 3.5 and 6.4 times, respectively, owing to the interaction of lutein and CDs. The ratio of lutein to CDs has been systemically investigated and the mechanism of the optimization of the lutein with CDs has been proposed. Our studies provide insights into how to protect and enhance the lutein and other supplements via the fabrication of a heterostructure, which may also shed some light on the food production and antioxidation.

The pristine lutein powder (purity of 90 %) and sodium copper chlorophyllin powder (purity of 99 %) were purchased from Xi’an Season Biotechnology Co., ltd. (Xi’an, China). Acetone and 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) were AR grade and purchased from Beijing Mreda technology Co., ltd. (Beijing, China). Citric acid and ethylenediamine were AR grade and purchased from Sinopill Beijing Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Beijing, China). All the chemicals were used without further purification and D.I. water was used throughout the work.

The carbon-dots were synthesized from citric acid and ethylenediamine in an autoclave, and the details can be found in the previous reports (Chen et al., 2021). The prepared CDs were dispersed in D.I. water to achieve 50 mg/mL for the modification of lutein. For the solvent-ultrasonic preparation, 30 mg of lutein was dissolved in acetone, and 0.3 mL CDs solution was slowly dropped. Subsequently, the mixed solution was continuously sonicated and then dried in a vacuum oven at room temperature for 4 h to obtain the solid complex LC0.5. In the same way, keeping the total mass of lutein and CDs unchanged in the mixed solution (45 mg), the LCs composites were prepared according to the mass ratio of lutein to CDs of 1:1, 1:2, and 1:3, denoted as LC1, LC2, and LC3, respectively.

The ultrasonic process was performed on a SK10GT ultrasonic meter (Kudos, Shanghai, China) at a consistent mode with a frequency of 53 kHz. Circulating water was used to keep the temperature at 25 ℃ during sonication. The sonication was performed in dark to ensure that the samples were not affected by sunlight. Each sample was placed in the same position during sonication for 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, and 90 min. Based on the photostability and antioxidant capacity tests, 60 min was selected as the optimal sonication time. The details can be found in the supplementary information (Fig. S1 A&B). In addition, the effect of ultrasound CDs for 60 min on LCs composites was excluded (Fig. S1C&D). The pH value of the mixed solution during the preparation was recorded (Fig. S2 A).

The pretreatment method of Cu-chlorophyllin (Chl) can be found in the supplementary. Lutein and Chl were dissolved in the same volume of acetone solution according to the mass ratio of 1:3. The mixture was put into the vacuum drying oven for 4 h after ultrasonic 1 h, and the L-Ch was obtained. The preparation processes were carried out at room temperature and in dark.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on an AXS-D2 diffractometer (Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) equipped with monochromatic high-intensity Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm), tube current 10 mA and tube voltage 40 kV. The scanning speed was fixed at 0.07°/s and the scanning range was 2θ = 5–80°.

Fourier transmission infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA). The samples were scanned in transmission mode and the spectra were recorded in the range of 4000–700 cm−1 with a resolution of 2 cm−1.
The water contact angle was measured by a JC2000D5M contact angle meter (Powereach, Shanghai, China). All the samples were deposited into thin films for the test at room temperature.
UV–vis absorptions were measured by a Cary-60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, California, US). The spectra were recorded in the range of 200–800 nm with a scan rate of 4800 nm/min.
The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were measured on a F-7000 (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) spectrophotometer with a Xe lamp as the excitation light source.

The photostability was measured under the irradiation of ultraviolet light from a high-pressure mercury lamp (365 nm, 15 W) or room light. The lutein, CDs, and LCs solid powders were dispersed into acetone to ensure the same weight concentration in each sample (0.04 mg/mL, 50 mL), which were exposed to UV or room light at room temperature for different durations. Generally, the intrinsic absorbance of molecules decreases when they are decomposed, and thus, the change of absorbance intensity of the sample under different conditions can be used to evaluate its stability (Hadjal et al., 2013; Deli et al., 2004). Photostability tests were monitored by a WFJ-2000 spectrophotometer (Unico, Wisconsin, US) at 447 nm of the fixed measurement wavelength. The lutein content (β) was expressed as followed formula (1) (Li et al., 2020):
β =
C t
/
C 0
× 1 00 %
where Ct is the absorbance of lutein after being exposed to UV or visible light for different time and C0 is the initial lutein absorbance of the sample.

Equal mass of lutein, CDs, or LCs solid powder was exposed to the air at room temperature in dark. Each sample was dispersed into acetone, respectively, and ensured the same weight concentration (0.04 mg/mL, 50 mL). The absorbance of the sample was also monitored by a WFJ-2000 spectrophotometer (Unico, Wisconsin, US) at 447 nm. The retention rate (γ) of samples was calculated with the followed formula (2) (Li et al., 2020).
γ =
A t
/
A 0
× 1 00 %
(2)
where At is the absorbance of lutein after being exposed to air for different days, and A0 is the initial lutein absorbance.

According to Singh et al. (2002), the antioxidant activity of the lutein, CDs, and LCs was assessed with DPPH free radical scavenging activity. Briefly, 0.06 mg/mL DPPH solution was first prepared by dissolving with acetone, and then, 1.0 mL sample solution (1.0 mg/mL) and 1.0 mL D.I. water was added to 4.0 mL DPPH solution. The solution was mixed and left at room temperature in dark for 30 min. The specific set of samples, base, blank and solvent groups were shown in Table S1. The absorbance of each group was determined at 517 nm. Three samples were measured in each group, and the average scavenging rate was taken. DPPH free radical scavenging rate (η) was calculated with the followed formula (3).
η =
1 -
T -
T 0
/
B -
B 0
× 1 00 %
where η is the scavenging rate of DPPH free radical, T, T0 , B, and B0 are the absorbances of the sample, base, blank, and solvent group, respectively.
The results were reported as mean ± SD (n = 3) (SPSS 18.0, SPSS Inc., Chicago, USA). All figures were drawn using OriginPro 9.0 (OriginLab Co., Northampton, USA).

FTIR spectra of the lutein, CDs, and LCs have been provided in Fig. 1A. The FTIR of lutein agrees with the previous report, with peaks at 2920 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1 belonging to the CH stretching vibration at the saturated hydrocarbon (Bolla et al., 2020; Boonnoun et al., 2013), and peaks at 1024 cm−1 and 963 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching vibrations and CH out-of-plane bending vibration from –CH = CH– group, respectively (Chen et al., 2009; Guo et al., 2021). The FTIR spectrum of CDs confirms its purity with the absorption peaks at around 1652 cm−1 and 1557 cm−1 assigned to the CO and NH stretching vibration in CDs, respectively (Wang et al., 2012). Compared with the spectra of lutein and CDs, the peaks of LCs combine those of lutein with CDs, and there are no distinct new peaks with LCs. The peaks at 2918 cm−1, 2850 cm−1, 1046 cm−1 and 964 cm−1 are corresponded to lutein, and the peaks at 1656 cm−1 and 1557 cm−1 are from the CDs. With the increase of CDs mass ratio, the peak at 1024 cm−1 shifts to 1046 cm−1 in LC3 (75 % (w/w) CDs), and the peak intensity at 964 cm−1 gradually decreases, implying the interaction between lutein and CDs (Toragall et al, 2020). The above results indicate that there is no chemical interaction between the lutein and the CDs (Huang et al., 2019), and whereas the lutein is successfully coupled with the CDs without damaging the pristine structure of natural lutein and CDs.

XRD patterns have been illustrated in Fig. 1B. Distinctive diffraction peaks of lutein are observed in the range of 10–27°, in good agreement with Huang et al. (2019), which confirms its high crystallinity and purity. For the CDs, the only observed broad peak is at ∼20.5°. The patterns of the LCs superpose those of the CDs with lutein, and the peaks of lutein decreased with the increased percentage of CDs, confirming the preserved structures of both lutein and CDs.

The water contact angle tests have been illustrated in Fig. 1C. The water contact angle of pristine lutein is 74.25°, which shows very limited hydrophilic properties, while the hydrophilic CDs show the contact angle of 16.15°. Compared to the pristine lutein, water contact angles of LCs significantly decreased with the increased percentage of CDs, showing that the modified lutein has become hydrophilic properties in varying degrees.

To evaluate the photostability of the as-prepared LCs, the degradation rate of lutein, CDs, and LCs have been measured under UV and visible light as shown in Fig. 2. In Fig. 2A, the color fading pictures with the degradation of lutein, CDs, and LCs under UV irradiation with different exposed durations have been provided. The colors of LCs retain noticeably longer than that of pristine lutein. The LC3 shows the best retention of the color, which visually illustrates the enhanced photostability of lutein with the modification of CDs.

Quantitively measured photostabilities of a series of LCs have been provided in Fig. 2B and 2C. Meanwhile, the pH value during photodegradation was monitored and its effect was excluded (Fig. S2B). After being irradiated under visible light for 18 h or UV light for 60 min, the Ct/C0 values of lutein decreased to 8.7 % and 8 %, respectively, which demonstrated the poor photostability of lutein. Compared to the pristine lutein, the photostabilities of different LCs were gradually improved with the increase of CDs. Notably, the LC3 showed the best photostability under both visible and UV light. Specifically, the Ct/C0 values of the LC3 achieved 70.3 % under visible light over 18 h and 11.2 % under UV irradiation over 3 h. However, after the mass ratio of CDs in LCs exceeded 75 % (LC3), the photostability of the LCs gradually decreased with the increase of CDs mass ratio (Fig. S3), and such reason for the decreased photostability of LCs is not clear yet.

The photodecomposition of pristine lutein, CDs, and a series of LCs were investigated by the pseudo-first-order kinetic model, and the curves were well linear fitted (Fig. 2D and E). The corresponded photodegradation rates can be calculated by the equation ln (C0/Ct) = kt, where the constant k indicates the degradation rate of each simple (Fig. 2F and G). The k values of the as-prepared LCs were all lower than lutein, and under both visible light and UV irradiation, the LC3 showed the lowest kinetic constants of 0.0272 h−1 (4.2 times lower than lutein) and 0.0108 min−1 (3.9 times lower than lutein), respectively, confirming the degradation mitigation of lutein-coupled CDs. In addition, to evaluate the retention of dried LCs, the lutein, CDs, and LCs have been deposited into thin films for the photostability measurements, which also confirmed the improved photostability of CDs-modified lutein. The details can be found in supplementary data (Fig. S4).

To explore the mechanism of the improved photostability of the LCs, UV–vis absorption and PL measurements were carried out. UV–vis absorption features were shown in Fig. 3 A. It is well-known that CDs show the absorption peaks at 246 nm and 338 nm, assigning to π-π* transition of CC band and n-π* transition of CO band, respectively (Fan et al., 2014; Pan et al., 2010). For the lutein and LCs, the peak at 447 nm was typical lutein peak (Silva et al., 2017). With the increase of CDs ratio, the absorbance decreased gradually. In addition, the PL properties of the dilute solutions were also measured after the UV–vis absorption measurements (Fig. 3B). By comparison, we can qualitatively explain the PL intensity based on the similar intensities of the three highest absorption peaks. Under the excitation at 447 nm, the PL spectra of the three samples showed obvious differences in the range of 450–600 nm, in which CDs exhibited the strongest PL. After lutein molecules were attached to CDs as the LC3, the PL intensity remarkably decreased owing to both the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and the electron transfer behaviors between lutein and CDs. It was for such transfer behaviors from CDs to lutein that the LC3 showed very weak PL intensity.

To further investigate the electron transfer and FRET phenomena from CDs to lutein, the PL spectra of the LC0.5, LC1, and LC2 were also investigated as shown in Fig. 3C. Compared to the LC3, with the decrease of lutein mass ratio, the PL intensities at 513 nm gradually increased under the excitation wavelength of 447 nm. It means that the increase of lutein molecules coupled with CDs causes the decrease of the PL intensity, indicating that the lutein molecules directly lead to lower PL intensity. With the increase of coupling lutein molecules, more electron transfer may take place from CDs to lutein, and thus the light response of lutein was reduced.

The stabilities of the lutein, CDs, and LCs in the air have also been evaluated and provided in Fig. 4 . The retention rate of the pristine lutein decreased to 3.6 % after 25 days since it is readily oxidized in the air (Qv et al., 2011), while CDs maintained a high retention rate (93 % over 25 days). Compared to the lutein, the retention rate of LCs was significantly enhanced. The LC3 showed the best stability with a retention rate of 68 % within 25 days. The obtained lutein/CDs composites exhibited excellent stability in the air owing to the protecting effect of CDs (Su et al., 2022). It facilitated the storage and transportation of products containing natural lutein.

The antioxidant capacity of the LCs was characterized and provided in Fig. 5 (red line). The DPPH free radical scavenging rate of the pristine lutein (1.0 mg/mL) was only 11.7 % within 30 min, while the CDs could scavenge 55.5 %. The LC2 and LC3 scavenged ∼ 66 % and ∼ 75 % of those at the same concentration, respectively, which indicated the significant improvement of the antioxidant capacity. However, when the mass ratio of CDs in LCs exceeded 75 %, the DPPH free radical scavenging rate of LCs gradually decreased. It may be interpreted that the excessive CDs attenuated the radical interaction with lutein.

As one of the chlorophyll derivatives, Cu-Chlorophyll has been proved to act as efficient antioxidants (Kang et al., 2018; Lanfer-Marquez et al., 2005; Zhan et al., 2014), antimutagens (Ferruzzi et al., 2002), cancer-preventing agents (Vankova et al., 2018), etc., which exhibited a positive effect on human health and has great applications in functional food and biomedical field. According to the previous reports, chlorophylls have a tetrapyrrole framework with some peripheral side groups and chiral centers, and thus they can be considered as a special kind of carbon dots (Ji et al., 2020). To compare with CDs, the coupling of Cu-chlorophyll (Chl) with lutein (L-Ch) has also shown significantly enhanced DPPH free radical scavenging ability (blue line in Fig. 5) with the same experimental method of the LCs. Therefore, the greener and healthier Chl can also be applied to enhance the antioxidant capacity of lutein.

A possible mechanism for the enhancement of antioxidant properties in LCs was illustrated in Fig. 6 . Both lutein and CDs acted as electron donors and participated in DPPH free radical reduction (Pérez-Gálvez et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2017). The formation of composites with lutein and CDs promoted the electron or hydrogen atoms transfer, and enabled the LCs to neutralize the DPPH free radicals. The enhanced DPPH free radical scavenging activity originated from the LC3, could be attributed to the antioxidant capabilities of lutein and the reduced contact between CDs and lutein (Chae et al., 2021), resulting in enhanced antioxidant activity with the assistance of the nanoscale carriers. With the increase of CDs mass ratio, the antioxidant ability of the LCs increased significantly, indicating that the enhancement of the antioxidant activity was mainly attributed to the combinatorial synergistic effect on the delicate interactions between lutein and CDs (Chae et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2022).

In this work, CD-modified lutein has been successfully fabricated via a simple, mild but effective solvent-ultrasonic method at room temperature, which significantly improved the photostability of lutein by 33.8 % under visible light, 11.2 % under UV irradiation, and extended its retention in the air by 68 %. By comparing the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of LCs with different CDs contents, the PL intensity of lutein was significantly increased by CDs, inferring that the electron transfer and FRET dual-behaviors occur from Chl to lutein, and the light response of lutein was reduced. Meanwhile, its antioxidant capacity has been enhanced by 6.4 times in the DPPH free radical scavenging measurements. In addition, contact angle tests confirmed that LCs composites had hydrophilicity in varying degrees. This study provided a new strategy for enhancing the environmental stability and biological function of lutein and other similar natural environmental-sensitive pigments, extending their applications in the pharmaceutical and food industries.

This research was supported by the funding of the Agricultural Environmental Monitoring Station of Beijing (No. 19000531813, 19000551279, and 19000551347) and Chemistry and Material Engineering College in BTBU.
Da-jian Jv: Investigation. Tian-hao Ji: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhemi Xu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Ao Li: Investigation. Zhong-yi Chen: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks for the DPPH free radical provided by Zhang Yongtao in the pre-experiment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134551.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
